# Cleaning Operational Text {#sec-chapter-02}

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Explain why oilfield shorthand breaks naive text search and NLP.
- Build a dictionary-based abbreviation expander that respects word
  boundaries so it doesn't corrupt unrelated text.
- Apply the expander across every `.txt` file produced in Chapter 1.

## Operational problem

Open one of the `.txt` files you extracted in Chapter 1 and read it as a
computer would: `POOH FR 9,842' TO CHANGE BHA. ERRATIC TORQUE OBSD LAST 3
STDS.` A drilling engineer reads that instantly. A keyword search for
"pipe" finds nothing, because the report never says "pipe" — it says
"POOH," "BHA," "STDS." Before we can search, summarise, or embed this
text, we need to expand it into language that means the same thing to a
human and to a machine.

## Example DDR extract

::: {.callout-note title="Sample DDR excerpt (DDR_018_EXAMPLE-1H.pdf)"}
```
0300-0700  STK PIPE @ 9,960'. WORKED PIPE, CIRC & PUMPED PILL.
0700-1100  FREED PIPE. POOH TO 9,500' TO ASSESS BHA CONDITION.
1100-2400  RIH TO 9,960'. WOC OVERNIGHT PER MUD ENGINEER RECOMMENDATION.
```
:::

## Theory

An abbreviation expander is just a dictionary lookup — `{"POOH": "pull out
of hole", "RIH": "run in hole", ...}` — but two details make or break it:

1. **Word boundaries.** `str.replace("RIH", ...)` will happily mangle a
   word that merely *contains* "RIH" as a substring. Match on whole words
   using regex `\b...\b`, not raw substring replacement.
2. **Case.** DDRs are written in all caps; expansions should read
   naturally in lowercase without forcing the rest of the sentence to
   change case.

This is deliberately not a machine-learning problem. A few dozen
well-chosen abbreviations, expanded correctly, solve most of the
readability problem — and unlike a model, a dictionary is auditable: you
can list every expansion it will ever make.

## Implementation

```{python}
#| eval: false
# code/chapter_02/expand_abbreviations.py
import re

ABBREVIATIONS = {
    "POOH": "pull out of hole",
    "RIH": "run in hole",
    "BHA": "bottom hole assembly",
    "NPT": "non-productive time",
    "LOT": "leak-off test",
    "FIT": "formation integrity test",
    "WOC": "wait on cement",
    "STK": "stuck",
    "CIRC": "circulate",
    "OBSD": "observed",
}

def expand_text(text: str, abbreviations: dict[str, str] = ABBREVIATIONS) -> str:
    for abbr, expansion in abbreviations.items():
        pattern = r"\b" + re.escape(abbr) + r"\b"
        text = re.sub(pattern, expansion, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return text
```

Run it against the text files from Chapter 1's `datasets/ddr_text/`
folder and save the expanded versions to `datasets/ddr_text_expanded/`.

## Practical exercise

**Try it yourself:** Run the expander against `DDR_018_EXAMPLE-1H.txt`.

**You'll know it worked when:** the line `STK PIPE @ 9,960'` becomes
`stuck PIPE @ 9,960'` and no unrelated word (e.g. any word merely
containing "rih" or "poo") gets corrupted.

## Challenge exercise

**Challenge:** Extend `ABBREVIATIONS` using [Appendix
B](../appendix/appendix_b_oilfield_glossary.qmd), and handle multi-word
source abbreviations correctly (e.g. distinguishing `WOC` from a
hypothetical `WOW` — wait on weather). A reference solution is in
`code/chapter_02/challenge/`.

## Key takeaways

- Word-boundary regex, not substring replacement, is the difference
  between a correct expander and a silently broken one.
- A transparent dictionary beats an opaque model here: every expansion is
  inspectable and testable.
- Cleaning text is not optional groundwork — it's what makes every later
  chapter's search and retrieval actually work.

## Repository files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `code/chapter_02/expand_abbreviations.py` | Word-boundary abbreviation expander |
| `datasets/ddr_text_expanded/` | Expanded output from Chapter 1's extracted text |
| `notebooks/chapter_02_explore.ipynb` | Interactive companion notebook |

## Suggested next step

With clean, expanded text in hand, Chapter 3 builds the first tool an
engineer would actually reach for: a keyword search engine that answers
"which reports mention stuck pipe?" in milliseconds.
